observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management consider carefully the nature of
transactions entered into and the relative implications of such transactions. Examination of the
City Government’s ownership or control over assets turned over to schools and other entities
should be made in order to gauge the proper treatment of those assets. If Management should
determine that it does not have effective ownership over the assets, the GSO and the
Accountant should drop them from the books for more accurate inventory reports and
financial statements. In implementing the foregoing, coordination should be done with the
Department of Education for them to appropriately record and monitor the transfer. Thereafter,
the GSO should issue an invoice and receipt for transfer of property instead of PAR or execute a
deed of donation where appropriate, while the Accountant should record the transaction as a
donation.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management follow the accounting
and reporting guidelines in the recognition of the various components of the Road
Network in order to ensure that the same is properly identified, valued and
accounted for in the books. The City Mayor should enjoin the department heads’
compliance with the circular, particularly the City Accountant, General Services
Officer and City Engineer.

We recommended that Management require the maintenance of complete
subsidiary ledgers for the account Due from NGAs-PhilHealth claims and regularly
update the same. Ensure that all claims from PhilHealth are correctly computed,
completely supported and filed on time to avoid losses on the part of the enterprise
for its non-collection.

We recommended that Management assess the conditions of its Accounts
Receivable and Other Receivables, prepare an accurate aging of receivables and
set-up allowance for impairment, if necessary, to reflect the actual net realizable
value.

We recommended that Management use the appropriate accounts as
prescribed in COA Circular 2015-009 in recording government transactions to
present fairly and accurately the financial statements.

We recommended that Management grant cash advances strictly for legally
authorized purposes only in accordance with COA Circular 97-002.

We recommended that Management carefully examine the duties and
responsibilities of the concerned employees and issue a certification that their
functions in their new posts/assignments are indeed comparable with the
supervisory or managerial nature of his/her regular position.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct all officers and employees to
register their daily attendance on the biometric machine and at the same time
maintain an alternative record book/logbook to support the manual entries in the
DTR for verification by the HRMO.

We recommended that Management reorganize the Internal Control
Division promptly. We further reiterated our recommendation that Management
review and revise the duties and responsibilities of the personnel, which must be in
line with the functions mandated by law. The Local Chief Executive should see to it
that the Division does not perform functions of routine operating character and that
internal auditors have no direct authority or responsibility for the activities they
review nor any responsibility for developing or implementing processes or systems.
Management should be guided by the Internal Audit Manual for LGUs in
establishing, maintaining and operating the IAS/U.

We recommended that Management require the Materials Test Results of
the infrastructure project as provided under COA Circular 2012-001, particularly
Item 9.1.1 thereof.

Auditor’s rejoinder:
The Audit Team pointed out that the cost of testing should be included in the
ABC, thus the winning bidder has no reason for non-submission of the materials test
results. Likewise, the Audit Team highlighted that any cost considerations on conducting
materials tests is inferior and should not outweigh the benefits that testing would accord
to infrastructure projects implemented.

We recommended that the City Mayor take the following courses of action:
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct the required annual physical count of the PPE in order to establish their physical existence and conditions and prepare a report thereon signed by the Inventory Committee and City Mayor.  The results thereof must be reconciled with the accounting records to effect any adjustments, as necessary. In addition, an Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable Properties should be accomplished to account for city properties that are already beyond economical repair to facilitate dropping of these from the books in case they are determined to be of no economic value in order to ensure that the balances of PPE accounts are fairly presented in the financial statements.
Require the General Services Officer to determine the end-users accountable for the unaccounted PPE items, require them to produce the same, otherwise, demand the return of the money value of the missing items.
Require the City Accountant to reclassify the PPE items in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units.
During the exit conference, Management articulated that they will exert utmost effort to comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that Management follow the accounting and reporting guidelines in the recognition of the various components of the Road Network. The City Accountant, General Services Officer and City Engineer must be mindful and perform their respective duties and responsibilities outlined in COA Circular No. 2015-008 in order to ensure that the Road Network System of the City Government is properly identified, valued and accounted for in the books.
Management stated during the exit conference that they will see to the proper inventory of the City’s local road network through the City Engineering Office and City General Services Office. Likewise, they will consider the guidelines in the COA Circular in the conduct of inventory and the subsequent recording in the books.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant and the City Budget Officer to use the proper accounts in recording transactions in compliance to COA Circular No. to 2015-009 and strictly comply with the conditions provided in the appropriation ordinance as reviewed by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan.
During the exit conference, Management expressed their willingness to comply with the recommendation. However, such compliance shall take effect only in CY 2020 since at the time of exit conference, the City was operating on the re-enacted 2018 budget where affected expenses were still budgeted under Other MOOE. Management also added that the IT Division was tasked to mail to all offices a copy of the Revised Chart of Accounts for them to be knowledgeable of the proper classification of their expenses. The departments/divisions were also advised to avoid using OMOE as much as possible as long as there are other more appropriate accounts.

We recommended that Management verify and validate the accounts receivable and other receivables account, prepare an accurate aging of receivables and set-up allowance for impairment, if necessary, to reflect the actual net realizable value.
With respect to the unpaid market rentals, the Market Superintendent stated during the exit conference that she will cause the closing of the said stall. Meanwhile, the City Accountant stated that they will endeavor to comply with our recommendation.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to submit the supporting documents of Accounts Payable listed in Annex “B” for verification and to revert all Accounts Payable not duly supported by complete documentation pursuant to Sec. 46 of P.D. 1177.

We recommended that Management facilitate the return of the amount of ₱3,347,500.00 from those who participated in the transactions, enjoin the compliance of the City Budget Officer and City Accountant to COA Circular 2015-009 and strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular 2012-003 and the Local Government Code of 1991.
Management stated during the exit conference that they shall take into consideration the observations and recommendation in future transactions.
Notices of Disallowance will be issued for the said transactions.

We recommended that Management:
procure common use supplies and equipment from the DBM Procurement Service and resort to Shopping or Small Value Procurement only upon compliance with the conditions provided for in R.A. 9184 and its IRR.  Procurements from other sources if not covered with PS Certificates of Non-Availability of Stocks are subject to disallowance as directed under Section 4.9 of DBM Circular Letter No. 2013-14; and
consolidate all procurement of common-use supplies and equipment in accordance with the approved Annual Procurement Plan to ensure efficiency in the procurement process.
Management, in their letter-reply, assured that they will try their best to comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team. However, Management stated that based on past procurements made in compliance to DBM Circular Letter No. 2013-14, DBM-PS does not carry stocks on most of the commonly-used items that they City was trying to procure therefrom hence resulting to procuring from local suppliers.
Management further stated during the exit conference that they have experienced previous issues when ordering with PS-DBM. When the agency goes to pick up the items at the PS Depot, the particular items ordered are no longer in stock and items of different specifications are offered in exchange.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee follow strictly the provisions of RA 9184 and its IRR and ensure that the Invitation to Bid, Notice of Award and Notice to Proceed are properly advertised in the required media and within the prescribed period to ensure wide dissemination of information and promote transparency and accountability in the procurement process.
Management signified their commitment to comply with our recommendation and informed us that the BAC Secretariat was advised to adhere to the posting requirements and timelines under the procurement law. Likewise, the BAC has already requested the IT Division to help in this matter. Management added during the exit conference that the IT has been tasked to develop a system wherein advertisements and notices will automatically be posted in the agency website upon successful posting in the PhilGEPS.

We recommended that Management revise its procedures relating to procurement and harmonize it to the provisions of RA 9184 and its IRR. Notices of Award should be issued by the City Mayor being the Head of Procuring Entity who has the discretion and authority to award contracts to bidders.
Management stated during the exit conference that they will comply with our recommendation.

We recommended that the BAC strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9184 and its IRR and be mindful of its duties and responsibilities thereunder. All procurement be meticulously and judiciously planned to ensure the smooth conduct of procurement activities, and that they are undertaken efficiently. The activities and procedures prescribed therein be followed strictly to guarantee the validity of the procurement. Likewise, the BAC ensure that the required notices are properly posted/advertised in keeping with the City Government’s commitment to transparency and accountability.
Management commented that they will strive to comply with the recommendation. With respect to the inconsistencies in the Bid Documents, the BAC Secretariat stated that these were mistakes that they will watch out for in succeeding procurements. For the posting of notices, BAC explained that there are instances where accessing the PhilGEPS is difficult but nonetheless will endeavor to comply with the recommendation

We recommended that Management submit a breakdown showing how the budget cost for the Rapid Response Vehicles were determined. For succeeding procurements, Management ensure that end-users prepare the ABC meticulously keeping in mind the cost considerations as prescribed in the Procurement Manual for LGUs. Likewise, the tools/equipment/accessories mounted on the vehicles be individually accounted for by the GSO to enhance accountability.
Management stated during the exit conference that they will comply with the recommendation and coordinate with proper parties to provide the details in arriving at the ABC.

We recommended that Management:
require the supplier to rectify its non-compliance to the Bid Proposal by replacing the installed strobe light with Whelen and deliver the accessories that were omitted as provided in the Technical Specifications,
submit justification for the following:
the difference in the color of the vehicle delivered with the color specified in the bid proposal
the omission in the Purchase Order of some of the accessories specified in the Purchase Request and Bid Documents
the inconsistencies in the supporting documents; and
determine and collect the appropriate liquidated damages incurred by the supplier due to delay and non-delivery of the remaining tools/accessories.
Management, during the exit conference, expressed their willingness to comply with our recommendation.

We recommended that the BAC competently perform the duties enjoined of them under the law and strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9184 and its IRR. In the conduct of pre-procurement conference, where appropriate, the BAC should discuss all aspects of the specific procurement activity, which includes the bidding documents.
During the exit conference, Management expressed their willingness to abide by our recommendations.
For the deficiencies noted in the procurement of the rapid response vehicles, a Notice of Disallowance will be issued.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:
No contract should be awarded to the supplier with incomplete supporting documents;
The BAC to submit the lacking documents and justify the lapses;
The BAC and TWG to examine all documents submitted and to recompute the NFCC to determine whether the supplier comply with and is responsive to all the requirements and conditions as specified in the bidding documents.
The BAC to use the forms prescribed under the most recent edition of the Philippine Bidding Documents.
Management to disclose all relevant information relative to the contract in clear and unambiguous terms.
For the deficiencies noted in the procurement of fuel, a Notice of Suspension will be issued.

We recommended that the BAC strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9184 and its IRR. After every failure of bidding, a mandatory review of the terms, conditions, specifications and cost estimates always be conducted to determine the reason for such failure. Any change in the mode of procurement be properly approved by the City Mayor and reflected in the APP. Likewise, the BAC ensure that the required notices are properly posted/advertised in keeping with the City Government’s commitment to transparency and accountability. Members of the BAC be mindful and keep themselves abreast with the procedures that must be undertaken in order to discharge their functions properly.

We recommended that Management:
exert all efforts to demand the immediate refund or return of the unrecouped advances or deduct the same from any claims due to the concerned contractors from the City Government; and
file the corresponding legal action in court for damages as a consequence of the breach of contract made against the City Government.
Management stated during the exit conference that they will look into the matter and will strive to comply with our recommendation.

We recommended that Management:
demand payment of liquidated damages amounting to ₱10,231.20 from Joshua & Caleb General Merchandise by reason of the delay in the delivery of the remaining unit. Incomplete delivery accepted as complete is irregular hence, may be subject to disallowance;
form an Inspection Committee to ensure actual inspection, verification and correctness as to quantity and specifications of the goods and/or services procured; and
adhere strictly to Item 3.1, Annex D of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184 in imposing liquidated damages to suppliers for delay in the delivery of goods and services and COA Circular No. 2012-003.
During the exit conference, Management stated that they will adhere to the recommendation and shall be mindful and strict in the acceptance of deliveries.

We recommended that Management review the existing policies regarding the operations of LCGH and, if warranted, apply necessary changes to improve its profitability. Consideration should be given to increasing profits by assessing the current pricing structure, especially items that decrease revenues such as discounts. Operating expenses should also be kept at a minimum. Otherwise, the LCGH should be discontinued as a local economic enterprise and reintegrate all its functions and services into the regular LGU operations in accordance with LBC No. 111.
In their comment to the above observation, Management lamented the recommendation of the Audit Team to “discontinue” operation of the LCGH. It also explained that there were events that hindered the LCGH from maximizing its earning potential especially in FY 2017-2018, which are:
Chronic lack of medical specialists to man the clinical departments of the hospital
Temporary loss of service ward/rooms to pave way for the expansion program of LCGH
Resultant status of disrepair of medical equipment due to incapable staff of the biomedical unit
Inoperability of the 64 slice CAT scan
Devastation of Typhoon Ompong
Combination of No-Balance Billing, All Case Rate scheme of PhilHealth, and Anti-Hospital Detention Law
In response thereto, the Audit Team clarified that the recommendation was not to altogether discontinue the operation of LCGH. Rather, the recommendation was to review the operational policies of the LCGH, with a view to increasing profits and decreasing expenses. If such measure is not undertaken or is unsuccessful, the LCGH is recommended to discontinue or cease being treated as a local economic enterprise and should rather become part of the regular operations of the City under the General Fund.
As to the factors cited that contributed to the poor earnings of LCGH in FY 2017 and 2018, the Audit Team is of the view that they can be remedied through adoption of appropriate measures, upon a thorough review of the systems and policies of the LCGH as we have recommended. However, it is worth mentioning that the issue of the financial viability of the LCGH is observable over an extended period and does not pertain merely to FYs 2017 and 2018.
Management also mentioned a triumvirate of laws and policies that affect the amount of revenues that the hospital generates (item 6). However, the Audit Team pointed out that PhilHealth compensated the hospital for its compliance to these laws and regulations and any losses could be addressed by an improvement in policy, Management and allocation of resources. Also, there were other discounts over and above those mandated by laws that have been given.
The Audit Team concluded that a thorough study and well-laid out course of action is needed from Management to achieve self-sufficiency for the LCGH.
In addition to the foregoing comments, Management stated during the exit conference that they will take drastic measures regarding the operations of LCGH to address the issue and that they shall take into consideration the DBM LBC No. 111. Management also stated that absorbing the operations of LCGH will lead to the City exceeding its Personal Services limitation. To this, Management appealed that the subsidy from General Fund be continued to help alleviate the conditions in LCGH.

We recommended that Management strictly observe the provisions of Sections 337 of RA No. 7160. Likewise, we recommended that the Finance Committee closely monitor the utilization of funds, adopt a plan in the allocation of available funds, formulate strategic measures to intensify collection of receivables, impose additional cost-cutting measures and exercise prudence in the disbursement of expenses to avoid further incurrence of cash overdraft.
Management stated during the exit conference that they will take drastic measures respecting the operations of LCGH to address the issue and that they shall take into consideration the DBM LBC No. 111.

We recommended that Management return the ₱6,000,000.00 from LCGH Funds to the General Fund – Proper and to refrain from utilizing appropriation for purposes other than its intended purpose to avoid disallowance and criminal prosecution for technical malversation.
Various irregularities in the granting and utilization of cash advance

We recommended that Management stop paying bonuses of employees through cash advances and instead directly deposit the amounts to employees’ respective bank accounts. Likewise, Management should ensure that the amount of cash advance granted to the accountable officers is within the limits of their maximum cash accountabilities. If greater cash advance could not be avoided, the amount of bond be first increased to an amount corresponding to his/her needed accountability.
During the exit conference, Management added that they have already complied with the recommendation and the recent payment of clothing allowance had been paid by directly crediting them to the employees’ respective bank accounts.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 regarding timely liquidation of cash advances and require the City Accountant to issue demand letters to officials/employees with unliquidated cash advances, otherwise, withhold their salaries as provided under Item 5.9 of COA Circular 97-002.
Management stated during the exit conference that they will comply with our recommendation.

We recommended that Management discontinue the practice of granting cash advances to an employee in behalf of other employees if not designated as disbursing officer for control purposes. In case a disbursing officer is designated for the cash advance, if payment is claimed by person other than the payee, that person has to present a written authority from the claimant and identification documents.
Management stated during the exit conference that they already implemented our recommendation

We recommended that Management facilitate the return of the entire amount of ₱318,195.00 for being irregular and illegal expenditure as defined under COA Circular 2012-003. Likewise, Management was advised to strictly abide with the provisions of COA Circular 97-002.
The Audit Team issued ND No. 2019-14-100 (2018) dated March 29, 2019 for the amount of ₱318,195.00 after Management did not provide any comment on the matter.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly comply with all applicable laws, rules and regulations in all its transactions and to exercise austerity measures in the disbursement of government funds to avoid disallowance in audit
For the procurement of plane tickets, the Bids and Awards Committee should recommend the use of the DBM-PS portal to obtain the most reasonable and advantageous price if not applicable, directly book to the airline company to avoid unnecessary expenses charged by the travel agency; and
Direct the City Accountant to ensure that all supporting documents are complete and valid.
A reply and the documents required were not submitted by Management within 15 days from receipt of the AOM. Hence, Notice of Suspension (NS) No. 19-005-100 (2018) dated March 26, 2019 was issued for the transaction.

We recommended that Management: (1) strictly comply with all applicable laws, rules and regulations in all its transactions and to exercise austerity measures in disbursement of government funds to avoid disallowance in audit; and (2) take necessary action against those liable in the alteration of the contract.
The Audit Team issued ND No. 19-013-100 (2018) dated March 26, 2019 for the amount of ₱133,000.00 because the hiring of the child star falls within the term “unnecessary expenditures” under COA Circular 2012-003.

We recommended that Management strictly abide to the provisions of COA Circular 97-002 in granting cash advances and require the issuance of official receipt from suppliers/contractors/professional upon payment by the City Government.
After evaluation of the comments, the Audit Team issued NS No. 19-003-100 (2018) for the amount of ₱290,000.00 and NS No. 19-004-100 (2018) amounting to ₱111,000.00, both dated March 26, 2018, due to the absence of the official receipt.

We recommended that Management:
discontinue the grant of travel expenses to RV G. Miguel as well as the additional honoraria to both employees for being irregular and having no legal basis or authority;
require to reimburse the amount by the responsible officers or employees who authorized the payment in the preceding paragraph; and
observe strict compliance with COA Circular 97-002 in the grant of cash advance. Only duly designated disbursing officers or employees are allowed for special cash advances by the explicit authority of the head of the agency.

We recommended that Management submit the authenticated copy of the following documents in order to provide basis in establishing the validity of all claims made in connection with the Manila Visitors Facility:
Contract agreement between the City Government and the lessor/owner Joselito T. Victor; and
Sangguniang Panglunsod Resolution or Ordinance authorizing the Local Chief Executive to enter into the Lease Contract.

We recommended that Management:
require the City Accountant to record transactions in conformity with their prescribed use under COA Circular No. 2015-009 so as not to mislead the users of the financial reports because of inaccurate recording and to exercise diligence in the processing of claims in order to ensure that all supporting documents are valid and proper;
require the refund of the tax due on the rental payments amounting to ₱12,400.00 and remit the same to the Bureau of Internal Revenue; and
enforce compliance with laws, rules and regulations in the disbursement of government funds.
Neither an answer nor documents required were submitted by Management within the period prescribed, hence, twelve (12) notices of disallowance were issued for the transactions totaling ₱481,441.90 for the reasons stated in Observations 12.1 and 12.2 above.

We recommended that Management ascertain the correctness of the reconciling items and prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) with supporting documents to adjust the amounts and report all bank accounts in the financial statements.
Management stated during the exit conference that they will comply with the recommendation and that they are exerting effort to identify and effect the adjustment of these reconciling items. Management further expressed that some of them have already been adjusted.

We recommended that the City Accountant establish the validity of the bank reconciling items in order to arrive at the correct balance of the cash in bank accounts.
Management expressed their willingness to comply with our recommendation during the exit conference.

We recommended that Management review and revise the duties and responsibilities of the Internal Control Division and align it to the functions mandated by law. The Local Chief Executive should see to it that the Division does not perform functions of routine operating character and that internal auditors have no direct authority or responsibility for the activities they review nor any responsibility for developing or implementing processes or systems. Management should be guided by the Internal Audit Manual for LGUs in establishing, maintaining and operating the IAS/U.
In response to the above, Management furnished the Audit Team a letter to inform us of the action taken relative to the issue. In the said letter, the head of the Internal Control Division informed the City Mayor of the mandated functions of the division and then proposed the reorganization of the division and the allocation of funds for the purpose.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant and City Budget Officer to cause the preparation of the reconciliation statement of their respective records and to furnish the Audit Team a copy thereof for evaluation.

We recommended that the agency refrain from paying overtime services rendered in excess of the regular working hours by employees without consideration of the viability of CTO as prescribed under CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2015 and avoid charging expenses not covered with an appropriation.
A Notice of Disallowance will be issued for the transaction.

We recommended that Management discontinue the issuance of checks to persons who are not the ones legally entitled to the claim through the scheme of naming his/her representative in the voucher as an alternate payee using a slash. Instead, the legal claimant should be the one named in the check and his/her representative submit documents to show a valid authority to claim the check, keeping in mind the documentary requirements provided in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
Management assured that in future similar transactions, they will comply with the recommendation and shall stop the practice of using the punctuation “slash.”

We recommended that Management adhere to the basic documentary requirements for local travel as stated in COA Circular 2012-001, Item 1.2.4.1 for local travel; ensure that all government vehicles be used only for public purpose; and travel orders and itinerary of travel provide with particularity the places visited and/or the events attended, in his capacity as a government official/employee.
During the exit conference, Management expressed that the concerned personnel have already refunded the travel expenses claimed. Likewise, Management stated that they shall comply with our recommendation.  The Audit Team also pointed out that the gasoline expenses need to be refunded as well. To this, Management acceded and stated that they shall compute the corresponding fuel charged for the travel to effect the refund thereof.

We recommended that the Accounting Office record prepayments of insurance premiums and other prepaid expenses as assets. Likewise, adjustment be made monthly to update these amounts and reflect the portions thereof that are expired as of the date of the monthly trial balance.
In their letter-comment, Management stated that they shall adhere to the recommendation in future transactions in the recording of prepaid expenses and use the proper expense account classification. Further, they informed the Audit Team that they have made corresponding adjustments in the books as of January 31, 2019 to comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that Management require the Materials Test Results of the infrastructure project as provided under COA Circular 2012-001 particularly, Item 9.1.1 thereof.
Management stated during the exit conference that they shall comply with the recommendation of the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management strengthen controls over all government property. The GSO should issue Inventory Custodian Slips for semi-expendable items given to officials and employees. It shall likewise maintain a file of said document for proper monitoring of those semi-expendable items and person accountable therefor.

We recommended that Management discontinue the granting of perks such as payment of mobile phone expenses to persons who are not employees of the City Government. The postpaid contracts of consultants be terminated. If pre-termination will result to penalties on the part of the City Government, the officials concerned in the grant and payment of mobile expenses in favor of consultants should be made to personally shoulder the amount.

We recommended to Management that payroll deductions be limited to the order of preference provided in the GAA and ensure that an employee’s net take home pay shall not be less than the minimum allowed take home pay of ₱5,000.00 as provided under Section 47 of RA No. 10964.
Management declared that once the concerned employees have fully paid their existing loans, they will strictly implement the recommendations of the Audit Team and shall ensure that the net take home pay of employees meet the minimum requirement of the applicable GAA for the year.

We recommended that the City Accountant revert the unutilized balance of DRRMF amounting to ₱8,385,456.78 to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund so that it shall be made available for other social services through legislative enactment.
Management commented that they did not revert back the balances of DRRM funds for CY 2012 and 2013 amounting to ₱1,112,576.43 and ₱7.273.880.35, respectively, to the unappropriated balance of the GF because they believe Sec. 5.1.13 of COA Circular 2012-002 requires a legislative act from the Sangguniang Panlungsod before reversion can be effected. On the other hand, the Audit Team argued that reversion is automatic after five years. When the LGU decides to utilize the same for other social services, that is when legislative enactment shall be needed.
To this, the City Accountant raised a concern that after reversion, the DRRMF might be comingled with other funds in the GF. However, the Audit Team clarified that the reverted funds are restricted for social services.

We recommended that Management require the:
City Accountant to make sure that all the data required by every document supporting transactions are completely filled up and observe strict compliance to Section 4 (6) of PD 1445;
Focal person on City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to submit the report on the determination of the areas affected by the calamities; and
City Social Welfare and Development Officer to fully account the relief goods procured immediately.
Management explained in a letter that the previous stocks for the Habagat had been used and distributed during the Typhoon Ompong and the immediate accounting of the same was not seriously made due to the unwanted coming of the Typhoon Ompong. However, Management promised that careful and complete inventory of undistributed goods shall be well done and given emphasis. Management further stated that they will religiously implement and comply with the recommendation in succeeding transactions.
The Audit Team duly noted the explanation proffered by Management. However, considering the amount involved, Management should have been more diligent in ensuring proper accounting of the goods purchased and distributed. Notwithstanding Management’s declaration to comply with the recommendation in future transactions, the instant observation necessitates action and compliance. Hence, the Audit Team issued two Notices of Suspension (NS) due to the absence of the distribution lists containing the quantity and item received and duly acknowledged by the intended beneficiaries/recipients. In addition, the transactions lack the report on determination of affected barangays and validated list of affected families as basis for the procurement of NFA rice.
During the exit conference, Management stated that persons indicated as responsible in the NS were already advised and are working on the updated list of beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to record in the agency books all procurement of relief goods procured under the Welfare Goods for Distribution account and issuance thereof to the Welfare Goods Expense account to reflect the correct balance of affected accounts.
Management expressed their willingness to comply with our recommendation during the exit conference conducted.

We recommended that Management prepare a LDRRMFIP with the details of the funded projects and activities for disaster mitigation, prevention and preparedness pursuant to Sections 5.1.1 and 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
Management articulated that they have already coordinated with the City Budget Officer, City Planning and Development Coordinator and the City Risk Reduction Management Officer so that the LDRRMFIP shall be prepared in accordance with the applicable COA circular.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to prepare and submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF promptly and regularly to the CAO as required under Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002.
Management replied that have been submitting the monthly utilization report for the past years. Unfortunately, though, submission was overlooked for 2018. Management requested that they be reminded by the Audit Team of their submission from time to time in case of similar situations in the future. They further committed to submit the monthly report as required.

We recommended that Management specify clearly in the AIP all the programs, projects or activities (PPAs) that are to be funded out of the 20% DF pursuant to DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1, in order not to deprive the intended recipients/beneficiaries of the services and benefits that could have been derived from the programs or projects indicated therein. Lump sum appropriation cannot be utilized without breaking it down into specific PPAs and only those specific PPAs approved by the Local Sanggunian have the authority of law and are therefore subject for implementation.
In their answer to our AOM, Management submitted an undated Schedule of Public Infrastructures funded under the 20% Development Fund for Special Assistance to Barangay (SAB) which appeared to have been prepared by the City Engineering Office on August 28, 2017. This document, however, is not sufficient to address the issue since it merely provides the list of projects to be implemented by the Management using the said Development Fund. It is true that these projects meet the total appropriation allocated to Special Assistance to Barangay amounting to ₱24,000,000.00. However, based on the AIP, the accumulated amount for all the specific projects planned to be implemented out of the ₱24,000,000.00 appropriation for SAB was only ₱10,445,941.96 leaving a balance of ₱13,554,058.04. Thus, the authorization from the Local Sanggunian for subsequent contract/s to be entered into by the Management is needed for the prosecution of the projects funded from the amount of ₱13,554,058.04.

We recommended that Management:
require the GFPS to prepare the GPB and GAD AR in strict compliance with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01;
use the Guide on the Review and Endorsement of LGUs’ GAD Plan and Budget and Review of LGUs’ GAD Accomplishment Report issued by the DILG and Bureau of Local Government Development to ensure successful compliance to the JMC; and
comply on the observations and recommendations made by the DILG - Ilocos Norte Provincial Operations Office.
Management answered that they will diligently comply with the recommendation and that they will fully conform with the provisions of JMC 2016-01 in the preparation of succeeding GPB and preparation of GAD AR using the required template/format and to always follow rules and regulations pertinent to it. They likewise vowed to religiously abide with the provisions of COA Circular 2014-001 in identifying and determining projects, programs and activities that are responsive to gender issues.

We recommended that Management fast track the establishment of the sanitary landfill as mandated under RA 9003 to ensure the protection of the public from health hazards and prevent environmental degradation.
Management asserted their commitment to fast track the establishment of the sanitary landfill.

We recommended that Management:
optimize the utilization of the 20% DF according to its intended purpose and undergo re-evaluation to determine what should be taken as a priority development project to be implemented and
expedite the implementation of all the identified development projects in order not to delay the services and benefits that could have been derived from its immediate implementation.
Management signified compliance to our recommendation during our exit conference.

We recommended that Management strictly follow the schedule of implementation of the programmed PPAs in the AIP so as to ensure the timely delivery of the services and benefits due to the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management ensure that the GAD AR is based on and consistent with the endorsed GPB, and to monitor the implementation of the PPAs responsive to GAD. If appropriate, in identifying GAD PPAs, Management should see to it that its successful implementation be fully realized or attained.
Management commented that they will fully implement gender-responsive programs, projects and activities in the GPB so that utilization of the allocated budget for GAD will be fully optimized.
We recommended that management submit the supporting schedule to aid us in the conduct of the audit of the aforementioned accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office is submitting quarterly and yearly schedules to COA as supporting documents to the monthly trial balance
observations.reason:
The schedules submitted are usually incomplete and submitted in installment basis, hence, the timely verification of the accounts cannot be made. Moreover, the schedule of Government Equity does not reflect the component of the said amount which is crucial in determining the accuracy and validity of the financial statements.

Accounts Receivable schedule does not contain the addresses of the debtor for verification purposes.

Accounts Payable schedule does not provide for the JEV number of each voucher covering the accounts payable recorded. APs found in the schedule are usually lumped and not itemized. Thereby, defeating the purpose of submitting the schedules.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management submit the supporting schedule to aid us in the conduct of the audit of the aforementioned accounts.

We recommended that management comply strictly with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 92-036, COA Circular 80-124 and New Government Accounting System for LGUs for the proper management and handling of the agency assets.

We recommended management to require the Accountant to:
Refrain from certifying to Accounts Payable claims not supported with complete documents and revert those that are not supported with valid claims and aged two years or more in order to ascertain that only valid accounts are included in the Accounts Payable account.
Submit all the remaining claim vouchers comprising the Accounts Payable account to complete our verification.

We recommended that management direct the concerned employees to:
Be more cautious in the performance of their assigned tasks.
Submit detailed listing of the Accounts Payable per disbursement voucher.
Effect the necessary adjusting journal entries in the books of the agency.

We recommended the City Treasurer to furnish the City Accountant a certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible at the beginning of each year as well as a list of real property tax delinquencies as of December 31, 2017 to record the actual receivables for real property taxes in the books of the agency.

We recommended that management send demand letters to recipients for long due accounts and exert utmost effort for their immediate settlement.  The funds collected will enable the agency to extend the same programs to other qualified beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Accounting Office observe the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards in the recording of transactions, install accounting control in receivable accounts and to prepare the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommended that management give preferential attention to the titling of the lots that are not yet in the name of the City Government pursuant to PD 1445 Section 39(2), require the City Accountant to reclassify the survey cost and taxes directly connected to the acquisition of the lot as part of the cost of the said lot and provide complete information on the four parcels of land for their proper identification.

We recommended that management require the City Accounting Office to:
Trace and verify the transactions pertaining to the reconciling items in Annex “B” and prepare a Journal Entry Voucher with supporting documents to take up adjusting entries corresponding to the reconciling items.
Submit documents to support the reconciling items such as Credit Memo, Debit Memo, etc. and attach to the next BRS the JEVs and supporting documents covering adjustments recorded from prior month BRS.
Indicate complete details of reconciling items for verification purposes.

We recommended that management direct the Treasurer to notify the payees upon approval of their check and for those payees/holders of checks previously issued which were not yet presented to the depository bank for payment one month prior to the date when said checks will become stale.

We recommended that management strictly observe the provisions of Sections 305, 337 and 344 of RA 7160, adopt a plan in the allocation of available funds, intensify collection of receivables and exercise prudence in the disbursement of expenses to avoid further incurrence of cash overdraft.

We recommended management to strictly comply with all applicable laws, rules and regulations in all its transactions and to exercise austerity measures in disbursement of government funds and to settle the disallowance covered by ND No. 18-003 pertaining to this transaction.

We recommended that management impose liquidated damages from the contractor for failure to complete the project within the contact duration as prescribed under Item 8.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and to monitor the 100% completion of the project.

We recommended management and the Local School Board to forgo the use of the Special Education Fund to purposes other than those prescribed by Section 272 RA 7160 and Section 4 of DEPED, DBM and DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended the Office of the City Accountant to determine possible causes of delayed/non-submission of vouchers and act on it in order to comply with the prompt submission of monthly receipts and disbursement records in compliance to Section 7.2.1 of the Rules and Procedures on the Settlement of Accounts otherwise, the City Accountant may be held administratively liable.

We recommended for the submission of the supporting documents required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 by the BAC, TWG and Secretariat members for the proper evaluation of the disbursements made for their honoraria.  Likewise, we recommended the City Accountant to ascertain that disbursements are supported with complete documentation.

We recommended management to require the submission of the liquidation report on the utilization of the P93,780.00 and supporting documents on the procurement of 200 monobloc chairs and table, compliant to Section 52 of RA 9184 and its IRR.   In the same way, we recommended management to follow up the submission of the liquidation report and supporting documents for the amount of P45,000.00 released to William R. Peralta on October 14, 2015, President, Federation of Senior Citizens Association of  Pasuquin, Ilocos Norte (FSCAP) for the purchase of monobloc chairs and  the amount of P20,010.00 released to Rex Epifanio Camaquin on November 10, 2015, Barangay Chairman of Barangay No. 32 Cabangaran, Vintar, Ilocos Norte for the purchase of diesel crude oil for the improvement of barangay roads.

We recommended that management insure with the GSIS General Insurance Fund all insurable assets of the agency pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-390 to enable the agency to recover from damage or loss of property due to unforeseen events.

We recommended concerned officials and employees to furnish the Auditor copy of contracts, purchase orders and their supporting documents within five days after perfection in adherence to COA Circular 2009-001 so that any deficiency noted in the review thereof could be immediately complied/rectified by management.  Otherwise, we recommend that appropriate sanctions be imposed on culpable officials and employees.
We recommended that management direct the Accounting Office to maintain hard copies of the Books of Accounts of the LGU for ready reference and sound management of public records.

We recommended management to require the City Accountant to ensure that an employee’s net take home pay shall not be less than the minimum allowed take home pay as provided under the General Provisions of the GAA per year.   For FY 2018, the prescribed minimum net take home pay is P5,000.00.  In addition, we also recommended that employees with deficiency in loan repayments as a result of the mandatory net pay to coordinate with the lending association/s for a direct payment arrangement.

We recommended that management replace the current OSCA Head and comply with the requirements of the IRR of RA No. 9994 in the appointment of new OSCA Head in order to avoid disallowance in audit and to give chance to other qualified senior citizens.
We recommended management to observe the provision of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014 in allocating funds for the LDRRMO.

We recommended management to comply with the budgetary requirement of one percent (1%) of IRA for the Local Council for Protection of Children and spend the budget for purposes provided for in DILG Memorandum 2012-120.
We recommended that management through the Focal Pont Officer observe the guidelines, procedures and timelines on GPB and GAR preparation and submission.
We recommended management to adhere to the provisions of PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Circular 2012-01 on personal services that could be charged to GAD.

We recommended management to utilize the unexpended balance for the intended purposes after due evaluation whether these are still priority concerns and to re-appropriate the funds of projects which have become irrelevant to the needs of the intended beneficiaries.
We recommended management to address the reasons for the non-use of the machines and equipment in order not to subject the resources of the government to loss due to wastage.
We recommended that the fund be utilized for projects in accordance with R.A. No. 7171 in order to provide timely benefits to the farmers.
We recommended management to demand payment from the Lessees for the amount due from them and to require the occupant of the two lots to pay rent.
We recommended that the fund be either returned or a request be made to the DTI, the source agency, that the amount be used for other projects/program beneficial to the SMES.
We recommended that the operating expenses of the Product Display Center be sourced from the income generated from its operations and not from the coffers of the City Government.
We recommended management to maximize the use of the GAD fund in order to attain the purpose envisioned by the creation of the fund.

We recommended management to direct the City Accounting Office to submit immediately the Subsidiary Schedule of General Ledger Accounts Balances to the City Auditor’s Office pursuant to Section 72 Volume I, NGAS for LGUs.

We recommended to management the following actions for the proper management and handling of the agency assets:
Conduct the physical count of the PPE as of December 31 of every year in order to establish the existence, condition and correct valuation of the PPE. Reconcile the result thereon with the accounting records and investigate discrepancies.
During the physical count, the inventory tag should be posted on a prominent portion but secured portion of the asset.
Submit not later than July 31 and January 31 for the first and second semesters respectively, the report on the physical count of inventory items.
Maintain and update supplies and property records for proper control.
Prepare and submit monthly lapsing schedule on depreciation of PPE accounts.
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to recognize the assets that have been dropped from the books and have not undergone proper disposal.
Provide budget for the titling of the lots in order to establish ownership.
Prepare adjusting entry duly supported with the necessary documents to derecognize the assets already transferred to the barangays.

We recommended that management require the Accountant to refrain from certifying to Accounts Payable, claims not supported with complete documents, to review all outstanding payables and revert those that are not supported with valid claims and aged two years or more in order to ascertain that the payables are valid and accurately recorded in the agency books.
We recommended to management the proper accounting of transactions in the agency books in consonance with COA Circular 2015-009.

We recommended to management the recognition of the acquired equipment in the books of the agency in accordance with the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standard, specifically PPSAS 13 – Leases for the fair presentation of the Medical Equipment and Finance Lease accounts in the financial statements.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended to the City Government to:
Cease and desist immediately from transacting business with GLEDCO in adherence to Section 108 of PD 1445; Section 1, Rule IX of RA 6713, and Item 1, Sec. 89 of RA 7160 and comply with the requirements of public bidding.
Submit the bidding documents for consignment of medicine and medical supplies with GLEDCO and Fuel Supply Arrangement; procurement papers for the supply of water refilling services, photocopy/printing services, dietary and laundry services;
Require the accounting personnel to quit from their functions with GLEDCO so as not to impair government function and avoid financial loss on the part of the government;
Submit properly and correctly accomplished drivers’ trip tickets and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption; act on the immediate repair or replacement of nonfunctioning odometers; and let all city vehicles be plated red.
Adhere to EO 821 and to the Drug Price Reference Index prescribed by DOH in order to deliver affordable and effective medicine to the public;
Strictly adhere to RA 9184 in the procurement of services as preferential right cannot be invoked when there is conflict of interest.
Bill the GLEDCO for the water and electricity consumption.
Submit the following documents to enable us to make a decision in audit:
h.1 Certified copy of the list of GLEDCO members and their respective investments in the cooperative for CY 2016.
h.2  Certified copy of GLEDCO plantilla for CY 2016.

We recommended management to comply strictly with the provisions of the IRR of RA 9184 on competitive bidding in order to obtain the best available contract at the most advantageous price to the City Government.

We recommended that management strictly observe the provisions of Sections 305, 337 and 344 of R.A. 7160, adopt a plan in the allocation of available funds, intensify collection of receivables and exercise prudence in the use of its resources in order to avoid further incurrence of cash overdraft.   Likewise, we required submission of the details of the Due to Other Fund account for our further evaluation.

We recommended management to require the City Accountant to:
Review the bank reconciliation statements prepared by his staff, analyze the reconciling items and effect immediately appropriate adjustments in the agency books.
Always provide the complete information and reference documents to validate the reconciling items in the bank reconciliation statements.
Record in the Accounts Payable stale checks or revert the amount to the unappropriated surplus those that remained outstanding for two years or more pursuant to Section 98 of PD 1445.
Likewise, we recommended that management require the Acting City Treasurer to:
Provide the City Accountant the list of the payees with unclaimed checks to record the Accounts Payable;
Comply with the procedures prescribed in Section 59 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs regarding stale checks.

We recommended management to allocate funds for the insurance with the GSIS General Insurance Fund of all insurable assets of the agency pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-390 and Republic Act (RA) No. 656   to avoid the possible loss and beyond recovery the cost of Assets and Properties in case of fire, earthquake, storm or other fortuitous event.   In addition, management is required to identify the uninsured 5 motor vehicles to include the same on the list of properties that needs to be insured and the Inventory Committee to conduct the required physical count of the agency’s properties in order to properly account all insurable properties.

We recommended that management require the immediate conduct of the physical inventory of its road network system and the 25% LRN be recognized in the books of accounts pursuant to COA Circular 2015-008 dated        November 25, 2015.
We recommended the City Accountant to re-classify the amount of                     P 54,666,666.68 of the Loans Payable-Domestic to current in compliance to the provisions of PPSAS 1.
We recommended that the agency comply with the guidelines on the handling and disposition of the host city share.

Concomitant to the foregoing, we recommended management to:
Submit the enumerated bidding documents in compliance to RA 9184 to avoid audit suspension/disallowance.
Require the supplier to refund/remit the excess payment of ₱ 8,332.50 which should have been withheld for tax purposes.
Submit a copy of the Memorandum of Agreement between the City government and DSWD.
Open a separate account for the SFP Fund as required in the Omnibus Guidelines and transfer the remaining amount of the fund allocation in the newly created account.
Submit copies of liquidation report.
Submit the pertinent documents to account for the ₱415,400.00 not reflected in the General Ledger for Due to Other NGAs.
Strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 as regards purchase of goods.

We recommended that management require the submission of the lacking documents for the Audit Team’s consideration and the Accounting Department to make sure that claims against government funds are supported with complete documentation.
We recommended the City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entry for the fair valuation of the asset accounts in the financial statements.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended to management the following:
To submit the lacking documents and comply with all the requirements stated in COA Circular 2012-001 Documentary Requirements for Common Government Transactions particularly the requirements in transfer of funds to NGO/PO to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit.
To stop the grant of Financial Assistance/Fund Transfer to NGO/PO with existing unliquidated cash advance from the City of Laoag.
To require the Accounting Office to record the fund transfer to NGO/PO amounting to P 275,000.00 as Accounts Receivable, to reflect accurately the balance of the account.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended management to:
Strictly adhere to COA Circular 2014-002 and 2015-009 in recording LDRRMF transactions. Use appropriate accounts and maintain proper inventory of goods for stockpile and distribution.
Submit justifications on the improper sequence of events/dates indicated on the documents.
Require the officials/employees in-charge in the distribution of relief goods/rice to provide for the quantity/volume of goods/rice issued to the beneficiaries in the distribution list.
Require GLEDCO, the supplier of goods used during typhoon Lawin to refund/remit the excess payment of P 72,830.36 to cover unwithheld taxes for the transaction.
We recommended that the Local School Board identify priority expenditures pursuant to Section 272 of Republic Act 7160 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular Nos. 01, 01-A and 01-B.

We recommended that management and the Local School Board discontinue the use of the Special Education Fund for non-priority expenditures to avoid disallowances and to be able to fully implement priority activities and projects envisioned by the creation of the fund.

We recommended that management discontinue any payment of allowances and other personnel benefits not in accordance with existing laws, rules and regulations and to adhere strictly to the pertinent provisions of RA 6758 and COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013.
We recommended that management exercise austerity measure and comply with the provisions of the travel law. The excessive and unnecessary disbursements should be refunded by the concerned personnel.

We recommended that the Office of the City Accountant submit immediately the lacking vouchers to facilitate the audit of the transactions. We have also recommended management to determine the possible causes of delayed/non-submission of vouchers and act on it in order to comply with the prompt submission of monthly receipts and disbursement records in compliance to Section 7.2.1 of the Rules and Procedures on the Settlement of Accounts.

We recommended that management require the employees responsible for accepting deliveries to notify the Office of the Commission on Audit of the time and date of scheduled deliveries. Unjustified failure on the part of the employee/s concerned shall be considered a ground for the automatic suspension of payment of this salary without prejudice to any disciplinary action that may be instituted against him (Sec. 122, P. D. 1445).
Management will comply with our recommendation.

We recommended that concerned officials and employees furnish the Auditor copy of contracts, purchase orders and their supporting documents within five days after perfection in adherence to COA Circular 2009-001 so that any deficiency noted in the review thereof could be immediately complied/rectified by management.  Otherwise, we recommend that appropriate sanctions be imposed on culpable officials and employees.
Management signified willingness to comply with our recommendation.

We have recommended management to maintain hard copies of the Books of Accounts of the LGU for ready reference and sound management of public records.
The management contended that all of the books of accounts are prepared and maintained thru eNGAS, an accounting software used to prepare and generate reports, nevertheless, they committed to comply with the foregoing recommendation.

For the foregoing deficiencies, we have recommended the following:

Create an Inventory Committee that will conduct the physical count of all PPE of the City in accordance with COA Circular No. 80-124.


Require the City Accounting Office to prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the properties that were not properly accounted in the books of accounts.

Require the General Services Office, the City Assessor’s Office and City Accounting Office to reconcile their records on the land owned by the City. Likewise, cause the titling of lots that are not yet in the name of the City in order to establish ownership through the City Assessor’s Office

Require the General Services Office and City Accounting Office to provide complete specifications so that the medical equipment and motor vehicles could be distinguished from among other properties. 

Require the General Services Office to accomplish the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property and comply with the procedure on the disposal of property provided under Section 379 of RA 7160.

April 29, 2016.

To be fully implemented in 2017
